status: DRAFT — NOT PUBLISHED — SME REVIEW REQUIRED
note: DRAFT end-of-course assessment for GP-445 pilot (WS2b). This course carries IICRC CEC hours (5) and its live WordPress description outline already ends with 'Microbe Clean - Basic Understanding Quiz', but no assessment currently exists — see GP-444 evidence. Structured identically to data/seed/floor-care-quizzes.json (courseSlug binding, quiz array, questions/options/correctIndex). DO NOT seed or publish until the founder (SME) has reviewed each question against docs/runbooks/sme-review-checklist.md and ticked this course off. Source: data/Microbial.docx 'Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course' course record (20 modules) plus data/wordpress-export/courses.json live description (slug match confirmed). Every question includes a sourceRef note field citing the docx module for SME traceability — sourceRef is a review aid only and is not consumed by the seed script.

quizzes.lessonTitle: End-of-course assessment: Microbe Clean — Basic Understanding
quizzes.title: Microbe Clean - Basic Understanding Quiz

questions.questionText: Why can a virus not multiply on a surface the way bacteria can?
options: Viruses replicate only inside a host cell and cannot reproduce independently on surfaces
Viruses are actually a type of bacteria
Viruses die immediately on contact with any surface
Viruses only exist in liquid form
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 1: Virus Facts

questions.questionText: Which bacterial form is described as the most resistant to standard disinfectants and requiring physical removal before disinfection?
options: Free-floating Gram-positive bacteria
Biofilm — bacteria embedded in a self-produced polymer matrix on a surface
Airborne bacteria only
Bacteria found only in soil
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 2: Bacteria and Modes of Transmission

questions.questionText: According to the five moments for hand hygiene in a cleaning context, when must hand hygiene be performed?
options: Only at the start of the shift
Before entering a contaminated zone, before touching a clean surface after handling contaminated material, after removing gloves, after handling waste, and before leaving site
Only if visibly dirty
Only once per site visit
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 3: Hygiene Practices

questions.questionText: Why must a surface be cleaned before it is disinfected?
options: Cleaning and disinfecting achieve exactly the same result
Organic matter neutralises most disinfectants, so disinfection is only effective after physical cleaning removes it
Disinfecting first always saves time
Cleaning is only cosmetic and has no bearing on disinfection
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 4: Cleaning and Disinfecting for Health

questions.questionText: What distinguishes a high-touch surface from a low-touch surface?
options: High-touch surfaces are touched by multiple people multiple times per day, such as door handles and lift buttons
High-touch surfaces are always made of metal
Low-touch surfaces never require cleaning
There is no meaningful distinction
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 5: Cleaning Types and Touch Points

questions.questionText: What is the correct cleaning direction to avoid re-depositing contamination on already-cleaned surfaces?
options: Work randomly across the site
Work from the cleanest areas to the most contaminated areas, and from high surfaces to low surfaces
Work from the most contaminated area outward in all directions
Direction does not matter if the same cloth is used throughout
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 6: Cross Contamination

questions.questionText: What is the minimum respiratory protection specified for environments with an aerosolisation risk from high-pressure cleaning or mould?
options: No respiratory protection is required if gloves are worn
P2 minimum
A basic surgical mask is always sufficient
Full-face SCBA is required for every job
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 7: Personal Protective Equipment PPE

questions.questionText: In the correct doffing (removal) sequence for contaminated PPE, which item must always come off last, and why?
options: Gloves — because hands are the dirtiest part of the body
The respirator — removing it earlier means removing airway protection at the point of highest contamination contact risk
Boots — because they are hardest to remove
Eye protection — because it is the cheapest item to replace
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 8: Donning and Doffing PPE

questions.questionText: What is a recognised sign of heat stroke that requires an immediate call to 000?
options: Mild thirst and light sweating
Core temperature above 39 degrees Celsius, hot red skin, rapid strong pulse, confusion or unconsciousness
Slight tiredness after a normal shift
A single sneeze
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 9: Heat Exhaustion and Heat Stroke

questions.questionText: Before starting an infection control or microbial remediation job, what should hazard identification specifically cover?
options: Only the client's verbal description of the job
Biological, chemical, physical and environmental hazards, using a standardised site entry checklist
Only chemical hazards, since biological risks are covered elsewhere
Hazard identification is optional for routine jobs
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 10: Hazard Identification

questions.questionText: Under Model WHS Act s.18, what must a documented risk assessment demonstrate for each specific infection control job?
options: That the PCBU has managed risks so far as is reasonably practicable
That the client has agreed to a price
That the job was completed on time
That no risk assessment is legally required
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 11: Risk Assessments

questions.questionText: Which sections of an SDS are identified as the priority for field workers before using a chemical for the first time?
options: Section 2 (hazard identification) and Section 8 (exposure controls and PPE)
Section 1 (identification) only
Section 16 (other information) only
The SDS has no prioritised sections
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 15: Safety Data Sheets

questions.questionText: Why must oxidisers, acids, and alkalis be physically separated in chemical storage?
options: It has no safety basis, only tidiness
Incompatible chemicals stored together can react — for example, sodium hypochlorite stored near acidic cleaners creates a chlorine gas risk
Separation is only required for chemicals of different colours
It is only relevant to office storage, not vehicles
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 17: Handling and Storage

questions.questionText: What must a worker do if a disinfected surface dries before the minimum dwell time has elapsed?
options: Consider the disinfection complete regardless
Reapply the product and restart the dwell time count, since dry application does not equal effective disinfection
Wipe the surface with a dry cloth and move on
Apply a different, unrelated chemical to compensate
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 18: Cleaning Procedures

questions.questionText: Under Model WHS Regulations r.299, what must an infection control business be able to produce on demand during a regulatory inspection?
options: A verbal explanation of past jobs
A copy of every SWMS relevant to the work, kept and made available to any person involved in that work
Only the business's ABN
Nothing — SWMS are internal documents only
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Microbe Clean Basic Understanding Course, Module 20: Safe Work Method Statements
